content:
# Exodus Chapter 32 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 101–103; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 75–76 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_032_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_032_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_032_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_032_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_032_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The People Demand a God: The Golden Calf (vv. 1–6)

**1** And the people saw [**vayyar ha'am**] that [**ki**] Moses [**Mosheh**] delayed [**boshesh**] to come down [**laredet**] from [**min**] the mountain [**hahar**], and the people gathered [**vayyiqqahel ha'am**] against [**al**] Aaron [**Aharon**], and they said [**vayyomeru**] to him [**elav**]: "Arise [**qum**], make [**aseh**] for us [**lanu**] gods [**elohim**] who [**asher**] will go [**yelkhu**] before us [**lefaneynu**], for [**ki**] this [**zeh**] Moses [**Mosheh**], the man [**ha'ish**] who [**asher**] brought us up [**he'elanu**] from the land of [**me'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**] — we do not know [**lo yada'nu**] what [**meh**] has become of him [**hayah lo**]."

**2** And Aaron said [**vayyomer Aharon**] to them [**alehem**]: "Break off [**parqu**] the rings of [**nizmey**] gold [**hazzahav**] that [**asher**] are in the ears of [**be'ozney**] your wives [**nesheykhem**], your sons [**beneykhem**], and your daughters [**uvenoteykhem**], and bring [**vahavi'u**] them to me [**elay**]."

**3** And all [**kol**] the people broke off [**vayyitparqu**] the rings of [**et nizmey**] gold [**hazzahav**] that [**asher**] were in their ears [**be'ozneyhem**], and they brought [**vayyavi'u**] them to [**el**] Aaron [**Aharon**].

**4** And he took [**vayyiqqach**] them from their hand [**miyyadam**], and he fashioned [**vayyatsar**] it [**oto**] with a graving tool [**bacheret**], and he made it [**vayyasehu**] a molten [**massekah**] calf [**egel**]; and they said [**vayyomeru**]: "These [**elleh**] are your gods [**eloheykha**], O Israel [**Yisra'el**], who [**asher**] brought you up [**he'elukha**] from the land of [**me'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**]."

**5** And Aaron saw [**vayyar Aharon**], and he built [**vayyiven**] an altar [**mizbeach**] before it [**lefanav**]; and Aaron called out [**vayyiqra Aharon**] and said [**vayyomer**]: "A feast [**chag**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**] tomorrow [**machar**]!"

**6** And they rose early [**vayyashkimu**] on the next day [**mimmocharot**], and they offered up [**vayyaalu**] burnt offerings [**olot**] and brought near [**vayyagishu**] peace offerings [**shelamim**]; and the people sat down [**vayyeshev ha'am**] to eat [**le'ekhol**] and to drink [**veshatoh**], and they rose up [**vayyaqumu**] to make merry [**letsacheq**].

---

## YHWH Speaks to Moses: The Decree of Destruction (vv. 7–10)

**7** And YHWH spoke [**vayedabber YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**]: "Go [**lekh**], go down [**red**], for [**ki**] your people [**ammekha**] have acted corruptly [**shichet**], whom [**asher**] you brought up [**he'eleyta**] from the land of [**me'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**].

**8** They have turned aside [**saru**] quickly [**maher**] from [**min**] the way [**hadderekh**] which [**asher**] I commanded them [**tsivvitim**]; they have made [**asu**] for themselves [**lahem**] a molten [**massekah**] calf [**egel**], and they have bowed down [**vayyishtachavu**] to it [**lo**], and they have sacrificed [**vayyizbechu**] to it [**lo**], and they have said [**vayyomeru**]: 'These [**elleh**] are your gods [**eloheykha**], O Israel [**Yisra'el**], who [**asher**] brought you up [**he'elukha**] from the land of [**me'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**].' "

**9** And YHWH said [**vayyomer YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**]: "I have seen [**ra'iti**] this [**et-ha'am hazzeh**] people [**ve'hinneh**], and behold [**am**], it is a stiff-necked [**qesheh-oref**] people [**hu**].

**10** And now [**ve'attah**], leave Me alone [**hannichah li**], and let My anger burn [**veyichar appi**] against them [**bahem**], and I will consume them [**va'akhallem**]; and I will make [**ve'e'eseh**] you [**otkha**] into a great [**gadol**] nation [**legoy**]."

---

## The Intercession of Moses (vv. 11–14)

**11** And Moses entreated [**vayechal Mosheh**] the face of [**et peney**] YHWH [**YHWH**] his God [**Elohav**], and he said [**vayyomer**]: "Why [**lammah**], O YHWH [**YHWH**], does Your anger burn [**yechereh appekha**] against Your people [**be'ammekha**], whom [**asher**] You brought out [**hotseta**] from the land of [**me'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**] with great [**gadol**] power [**bekoach**] and with a mighty [**chazaqah**] hand [**uveyad**]?

**12** Why [**lammah**] should the Egyptians [**Mitsrayim**] say [**yomeru**], saying [**le'mor**]: 'With evil intent [**bera'ah**] He brought them out [**hotsi'am**], to kill them [**laharog otam**] in the mountains [**beharim**] and to consume them [**ulekhalotam**] from upon [**me'al**] the face of [**peney**] the ground [**ha'adamah**]'? Turn [**shuv**] from the burning of [**meacharon**] Your anger [**appekha**], and relent [**vehinnahem**] concerning the evil [**al-hara'ah**] against Your people [**le'ammekha**].

**13** Remember [**zekhor**] Abraham [**le'Avraham**], Isaac [**leYitschaq**], and Israel [**uleYisra'el**], Your servants [**avadekha**], to whom [**asher**] You swore [**nishbata**] by Yourself [**lakh**], and You spoke [**vatedabber**] to them [**alehem**]: 'I will multiply [**arbeh**] your seed [**et-zar'akhem**] as the stars of [**kekhokh'vey**] the heavens [**hashamayim**], and all [**vekhol**] this [**hazzot**] land [**ha'arets**] that [**asher**] I have spoken of [**amarti**] I will give [**etten**] to your seed [**lezar'akhem**], and they shall inherit it [**venachalu**] forever [**le'olam**].' "

**14** And YHWH relented [**vayyinnachem YHWH**] concerning the evil [**al-hara'ah**] which [**asher**] He had spoken [**dibber**] to do [**la'asot**] to His people [**le'ammo**].

---

## Moses Descends: The Tablets Shattered (vv. 15–20)

**15** And Moses turned [**vayyifen**] and went down [**vayyered**] from [**min**] the mountain [**hahar**], and the two [**ushney**] tablets of [**luchot**] the Testimony [**ha'edut**] were in his hand [**beyado**] — tablets [**luchot**] written [**ketuvim**] on both their sides [**mishney evreyhem**]; on this side [**mizzeh**] and on that side [**umizzeh**] they were written [**hem ketuvim**].

**16** And the tablets [**vehaluchot**] were the work of [**ma'aseh**] God [**Elohim**] they were [**hemmah**], and the writing [**vehamikhtav**] was the writing of [**mikhtav**] God [**Elohim**] it was [**hu**], engraved [**charut**] upon [**al**] the tablets [**halluchot**].

**17** And Joshua [**Yehoshua**] heard [**vayyishma**] the sound of [**et qol**] the people [**ha'am**] in their shouting [**bere'oh**], and he said [**vayyomer**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**]: "A sound of [**qol**] war [**milchamah**] is in the camp [**bammachaneh**]!"

**18** And he said [**vayyomer**]: "It is not [**eyn**] the sound of [**qol**] a shout of [**anot**] victory [**gevurah**], and it is not [**ve'eyn**] the sound of [**qol**] a shout of [**anot**] defeat [**chalushah**]; the sound of [**qol**] singing [**annot**] I [**anokhi**] hear [**shomea**]."

**19** And it came to pass [**vayhi**], as he drew near [**ka'asher qarav**] to [**el**] the camp [**hammachaneh**], that he saw [**vayyar**] the calf [**et-ha'egel**] and the dancing [**umecholot**]; and the anger of [**vayyichar-af**] Moses burned [**Mosheh**], and he cast [**vayyashlekh**] the tablets [**et-halluchot**] from his hands [**miyadav**], and he shattered them [**vayshabber otam**] at the foot of [**tachat**] the mountain [**hahar**].

**20** And he took [**vayyiqqach**] the calf [**et-ha'egel**] which [**asher**] they had made [**asu**], and he burned it [**vayyisrof**] in the fire [**ba'esh**], and he ground it [**vayyitchan**] until [**ad**] it was fine [**asher-daq**], and he scattered [**vayyizer**] it upon [**al-peney**] the face of the waters [**hammayim**], and he made the Sons of Israel [**vayyashq et-beney Yisra'el**] drink it.

---

## The Confrontation with Aaron (vv. 21–24)

**21** And Moses said [**vayyomer Mosheh**] to [**el**] Aaron [**Aharon**]: "What [**meh**] did this [**hazzeh**] people [**ha'am**] do [**asah**] to you [**lakh**], that [**ki**] you have brought [**heveta**] upon it [**alav**] a great [**gedolah**] sin [**chata'ah**]?"

**22** And Aaron said [**vayyomer Aharon**]: "Let not [**al**] the anger [**af**] of my lord [**adoni**] burn [**yichar**]. You [**attah**] know [**yadata**] the people [**et-ha'am**], that [**ki**] it is [**hu**] set on evil [**vera**].

**23** And they said [**vayyomeru**] to me [**li**]: 'Make [**aseh**] for us [**lanu**] gods [**elohim**] who [**asher**] will go [**yelkhu**] before us [**lefaneynu**], for [**ki**] this [**zeh**] Moses [**Mosheh**], the man [**ha'ish**] who [**asher**] brought us up [**he'elanu**] from the land of [**me'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**] — we do not know [**lo yada'nu**] what [**meh**] has become of him [**hayah lo**].'

**24** And I said [**va'omar**] to them [**lahem**]: 'Whoever [**lemi**] has gold [**zahav**] — break it off [**hitparqu**]!' And they gave it [**vayyittenu**] to me [**li**], and I cast it [**va'ashlikhehu**] into the fire [**ba'esh**], and out came [**vayyetse**] this [**hazzeh**] calf [**ha'egel**]."

---

## The Zeal of the Levites (vv. 25–29)

**25** And Moses saw [**vayyar Mosheh**] the people [**et-ha'am**], that [**ki**] it was unrestrained [**parua hu**], for [**ki**] Aaron had let them loose [**fera'oh Aharon**] to a whispering [**leshimtsah**] among those who rose against them [**beqameyhem**].

**26** And Moses stood [**vayyaamod Mosheh**] in the gate of [**besha'ar**] the camp [**hammachaneh**] and said [**vayyomer**]: "Whoever [**mi**] is for YHWH [**laYHWH**], come to me [**elay**]!" And all [**kol**] the Sons of [**beney**] Levi [**Levi**] gathered [**vayyeasefu**] to him [**elav**].

**27** And he said [**vayyomer**] to them [**lahem**]: "Thus [**koh**] says [**amar**] YHWH [**YHWH**], the God of [**Elohey**] Israel [**Yisra'el**]: 'Put [**simu**] each man [**ish**] his sword [**charbo**] upon [**al**] his thigh [**yerekho**]; pass back and forth [**ivru vashuvu**] from gate [**misha'ar**] to gate [**lasha'ar**] in the camp [**bammachaneh**], and kill [**vehirgu**] each man [**ish**] his brother [**et-achiv**], and each man [**ve'ish**] his companion [**et-re'ehu**], and each man [**ve'ish**] his neighbor [**et-qerovo**].' "

**28** And the Sons of [**beney**] Levi [**Levi**] did [**vayyaasu**] according to the word of [**kidvar**] Moses [**Mosheh**]; and there fell [**vayyippol**] from the people [**min-ha'am**] on that [**ba**] day [**yom hahu**] about three thousand [**kisheloshet alfey**] men [**ish**].

**29** And Moses said [**vayyomer Mosheh**]: "Consecrate yourselves [**mil'u yedkhem**] today [**hayyom**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**], for [**ki**] each man [**ish**] was against his son [**bivno**] and against his brother [**uve'achiv**]; and so [**velatet**] He may bestow [**aleykhem**] upon you [**hayyom**] today [**berakhah**] a blessing."

---

## Moses Returns to YHWH: The Offer of Atonement (vv. 30–35)

**30** And it came to pass [**vayhi**] on the next day [**mimmocharot**], and Moses said [**vayyomer Mosheh**] to [**el**] the people [**ha'am**]: "You [**attem**] — you have sinned [**chatatem**] a great [**gedolah**] sin [**chata'ah**]; and now [**ve'attah**] I will go up [**e'eleh**] to [**el**] YHWH [**YHWH**] — perhaps [**ulay**] I can make atonement [**akhaparah**] for [**be'ad**] your sin [**chattatchem**]."

**31** And Moses returned [**vayyashov Mosheh**] to [**el**] YHWH [**YHWH**] and said [**vayyomer**]: "Alas [**anna**], this [**hazzeh**] people [**ha'am**] has sinned [**chata**] a great [**gedolah**] sin [**chata'ah**], and they have made [**vayyaasu**] for themselves [**lahem**] gods of [**elohey**] gold [**zahav**].

**32** And now [**ve'attah**], if [**im**] You will forgive [**tissa**] their sin [**chattam**] — ; and if [**ve'im**] not [**ayin**], please blot me out [**mecheni na**] from Your book [**missifrekha**] which [**asher**] You have written [**katavta**]."

**33** And YHWH said [**vayyomer YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**]: "Whoever [**mi asher**] has sinned [**chata**] against Me [**li**], I will blot him out [**emchennu**] from My book [**missifri**].

**34** And now [**ve'attah**], go [**lekh**], lead [**necheh**] the people [**et-ha'am**] to [**el**] the place of which [**asher**] I spoke [**dibbarti**] to you [**lakh**]. Behold [**hinneh**], My angel [**malakhi**] will go [**yelekh**] before you [**lefanekha**]; and on the day [**uveyom**] of My visitation [**poqdi**], I will visit [**ufaqadti**] their sin [**chattatam**] upon them [**alehem**]."

**35** And YHWH struck [**vayyiggof YHWH**] the people [**et-ha'am**] because [**al asher**] they had made [**asu**] the calf [**et-ha'egel**] which [**asher**] Aaron had made [**asah Aharon**].

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Key Terms

**boshesh** (v. 1) -- "delayed." The verb boshesh (from b-w-sh or b-sh-sh, "to be ashamed, to delay, to be long in coming") is a rare Polel form appearing only here and in Judges 5:28, where Sisera's mother asks why his chariot "delays" (boshesh) in returning. The semantic field encompasses both temporal delay and shame: Moses is "shamefully late" in the people's perception. The Talmud (b. Shabbat 89a) connects boshesh with ba'u shesh sha'ot ("six hours had come"), interpreting the people's impatience as calculated from a specific deadline they expected for Moses's return. The people's demand arises not from months of waiting (Moses has been on the mountain forty days, Exod 24:18) but from a perceived breach of his promised return. The LXX renders this as kechōniken ("delayed, lingered"), using a form of chronizō ("to take time, to tarry"), which captures the temporal sense but not the shame connotation.

**elohim asher yelkhu lefaneynu** (v. 1) -- "gods who will go before us." The demand for elohim ("gods" or possibly "a god") asher yelkhu ("who will go") lefaneynu ("before us") reflects the ancient Near Eastern concept of the divine image that leads a people in procession and battle. The plural verb yelkhu ("they will go") with elohim may indicate that the people request genuine polytheistic representation, though elohim with plural verbs can also refer to a single deity construed as a plural of majesty. The phrase lefaneynu ("before our faces") is the language of military and processional leadership, the same function YHWH Himself performs with the pillar of cloud and fire (Exod 13:21). The people are not merely seeking a replacement for Moses but a replacement for the visible manifestation of YHWH's presence. The LXX renders this as poiēson hēmin theous hoi proporeuontai hēmōn ("make for us gods who will go before us"), using the plural theous ("gods") and the plural verb proporeuontai.

**egel massekah** (v. 4) -- "a molten calf." The noun egel means "a young bull, calf," and massekah (from n-s-k, "to pour, to cast") means "a cast image, a molten image." The combination describes a metal image formed by pouring molten gold into a mold. The calf/young bull was the primary symbol of the Canaanite storm god Baal/Hadad and also of the Egyptian bull-deity Apis (Hapi). The choice of a bovine image likely reflects both Egyptian religious influence (the people have just come from Egypt) and the broader Canaanite cultural milieu into which they are entering. The same term egel massekah does not recur, but Jeroboam's golden calves at Dan and Bethel (1 Kgs 12:28) deliberately echo this episode, using the identical proclamation: "Behold your gods, O Israel, who brought you up from the land of Egypt." The verb vayyatsar ("and he fashioned") is the same verb used for YHWH's formation of man from the dust (Gen 2:7, vayyitser), creating a dark parody: Aaron "forms" an idol with the same creative verb by which YHWH formed humanity. The LXX renders egel massekah as moschos chōneutos ("a cast/molten calf"), using chōneutos from chōneuō ("to smelt, to cast in a mold").

**elleh eloheykha Yisra'el** (v. 4) -- "These are your gods, O Israel." The demonstrative pronoun elleh ("these") is plural, and eloheykha ("your gods") bears a plural suffix, yet the referent is a single calf. This grammatical plurality has generated extensive discussion. Some scholars argue the narrator preserves an older tradition in which multiple images were made; others note that elohim with plural modifiers can refer to a single deity (cf. 1 Sam 4:8; Neh 9:18). The phrase may also be a later editorial alignment with the Jeroboam narrative (1 Kgs 12:28), where two calves justify the plural. In the MT's present form, the jarring plural applied to one calf magnifies the theological absurdity of the act: a single golden image is addressed as though it were the mighty divine power(s) that defeated Egypt. The LXX follows the MT's plurality: houtoi hoi theoi sou, Israēl ("these are your gods, O Israel").

**vayyechal Mosheh et-peney YHWH** (v. 11) -- "And Moses entreated the face of YHWH." The verb chalah (Piel: "to entreat, to make the face smooth, to soften the countenance") is the technical term for urgent, fervent supplication. The idiom "entreating the face" (challot panim) pictures the petitioner seeking to change the facial expression of the ruler from anger to favor, from severity to mercy. This is the first instance in the Torah of a human interceding with God to avert judgment from a people, establishing Moses as the archetypal mediator and intercessor. The LXX renders this as kai edeēthē Mōusēs enanti kuriou tou theou ("and Moses besought before the Lord God"), using deomai ("to beseech, to entreat"), which captures the urgency but loses the vivid "face" imagery.

**hannichah li** (v. 10) -- "Leave Me alone" or "Let Me be." The imperative hannichah (Hiphil of n-w-ch, "to rest, to leave") with the dative li ("to/for Me") is an extraordinary phrase: YHWH asks Moses's permission, as it were, to destroy the people. The rabbinical tradition (Exodus Rabbah 42:9) observed that this phrasing implies its own reversal: by telling Moses "leave Me alone," YHWH is signaling that Moses should not leave Him alone -- that intercession is possible and invited. If YHWH truly intended unilateral destruction, He would not have disclosed His intent to Moses at all. The phrase thus functions as a paradoxical invitation to intercede, embedded within a declaration of wrath. The LXX renders this as eason me ("allow Me, let Me be"), which preserves the sense of YHWH requesting non-interference.

**mil'u yedkhem** (v. 29) -- "Consecrate yourselves," literally "Fill your hands." The idiom mille' yad ("to fill the hand") is the technical expression for priestly ordination (cf. Exod 28:41; 29:9, 33, 35; Lev 8:33). Its use here is profoundly significant: the Levites' act of judgment against the calf-worshippers functions as their priestly ordination. They "fill their hands" with the sword rather than with the sacrificial portions, and this act of zeal for YHWH becomes the basis of the Levitical priesthood's election (cf. Deut 33:8-11). The cost of ordination is devastating -- "each man against his son and against his brother" -- but this willingness to place covenant loyalty above kinship loyalty is precisely what qualifies them for priestly service. The LXX renders this as eplērōsate tas cheiras humōn sēmeron tō kuriō ("you have filled your hands today to the Lord").

**im-tissa chattatam... ve'im-ayin mecheni na missifrekha** (v. 32) -- "If You will forgive their sin -- ; and if not, blot me out from Your book." This is among the most extraordinary utterances in the Hebrew Bible. The protasis ("if You will forgive their sin") is left without an apodosis -- the sentence breaks off, indicated in the MT by a long dash or empty space. Moses cannot bring himself to complete the conditional: the blessing that would follow forgiveness is too great for words, or too presumptuous to speak. The alternative is staggering: Moses offers his own life -- his name in the divine book -- as a substitute for the people's sin. The "book" (sefer) that YHWH has "written" (katavta) is the book of life, the divine register of those who belong to YHWH (cf. Ps 69:29 [Heb 69:28]; Dan 12:1; Mal 3:16). To be "blotted out" (machah, Qal imperative with paragogic nun: mecheni) from this book is to be erased from existence itself, from divine memory and covenant belonging. Moses's offer anticipates and exceeds any other intercessory self-sacrifice in the Hebrew Bible, and the New Testament will see in it a type of Christ's substitutionary offering (cf. Rom 9:3, where Paul expresses a similar willingness to be "accursed" for the sake of his kinsmen). The LXX renders this as kai nun ei men apheis autois tēn hamartian autōn, aphes; ei de mē, exaleipson me ek tēs biblou sou hēs egrapsas ("and now if you will forgive them their sin, forgive; but if not, blot me out of your book which you have written"), filling in the broken apodosis with aphes ("forgive"), which smooths the syntactic rupture of the Hebrew.

### Grammatical Notes

**vayyiqqahel ha'am al Aharon** (v. 1) -- "the people gathered against Aaron." The verb qahal (Niphal: vayyiqqahel, "gathered themselves") with the preposition al ("upon, against") carries a hostile connotation: the people assemble not merely around Aaron but against him, in confrontation. The Niphal stem indicates reflexive action -- they gathered themselves -- suggesting a spontaneous mob rather than an orderly assembly. The same construction (qahal + al) appears in Numbers 16:3 and 20:2 for rebellious assemblies against Moses and Aaron, establishing it as the standard vocabulary for seditious gathering.

**vayyatsar oto bacheret** (v. 4) -- "and he fashioned it with a graving tool." The verb yatsar ("to form, fashion, shape") in the Qal takes the direct object oto ("it") and the instrumental bacheret ("with a graving tool" or "with a stylus"). The noun cheret appears only here and in Isaiah 8:1, where it refers to a writing instrument. The exact process described is debated: did Aaron first cast the gold in a mold (massekah, "molten image") and then refine it with an engraving tool (cheret), or did he bind/wrap (from ts-r-r, "to bind") the gold in a cloth bag (cheret from cher-et, "bag, purse") before casting? The MT's vocalization favors the "graving tool" reading. The LXX renders this as kai eplasen auta en tē graphidi ("and he shaped them with the stylus/graving tool"), using graphidi (from graphis, "stylus"), supporting the "engraving tool" interpretation.

**chag laYHWH machar** (v. 5) -- "A feast to YHWH tomorrow." Aaron's declaration reveals his attempt to syncretize the golden calf with YHWH worship. He does not declare a feast "to the calf" or "to other gods" but laYHWH, "to YHWH" -- attempting to present the calf as a legitimate representation of YHWH rather than an alternative deity. This syncretistic intent makes the sin more subtle and more dangerous than outright idolatry: it is the worship of YHWH through a forbidden image, the blending of true worship with pagan form.

**letsacheq** (v. 6) -- "to make merry" or "to play." The verb tsachaq (Piel: letsacheq, "to play, to sport, to make merry") encompasses a wide semantic range from innocent play (Gen 21:9) to sexual activity (Gen 26:8; 39:14, 17). In context, the Piel intensifies the action beyond simple celebration. The juxtaposition with eating and drinking, the removal of restraint (v. 25, parua, "let loose"), and the "dancing" (mecholot, v. 19) suggest orgiastic revelry typical of ancient Near Eastern fertility cult worship. Paul cites this verse in 1 Corinthians 10:7 (epaizen, "they played") as a warning against idolatry, and the broader context of 1 Corinthians 10:1-13 treats the golden calf episode as paradigmatic for Christian ethical instruction. The LXX renders this as paizein ("to play, to sport").

**saru maher min-hadderekh** (v. 8) -- "they have turned aside quickly from the way." The verb sur ("to turn aside, to depart") with maher ("quickly") emphasizes the rapidity of Israel's apostasy. The "way" (derekh) from which they have turned is both the specific commandment against images (Exod 20:4-6; 20:23) and the entire covenantal path (cf. Deut 9:12, 16, identical language). The adverb maher ("quickly") is devastating: after only forty days, the people have abandoned what they solemnly swore at Sinai (Exod 24:3, 7). The same verb sur ("turn aside") appears in Deuteronomy's standard apostasy formula (Deut 11:16, 28; 31:29).

**vayyinnachem YHWH al-hara'ah** (v. 14) -- "And YHWH relented concerning the evil." The verb nacham (Niphal: vayyinnachem, "He relented, He was comforted, He changed His mind") is one of the most theologically charged verbs in the Hebrew Bible when predicated of YHWH. The same verb appears in Genesis 6:6 ("YHWH was grieved that He had made man") and 1 Samuel 15:11, 35 ("I regret that I made Saul king"), yet 1 Samuel 15:29 declares that "the Glory of Israel does not lie or relent (yinnachem), for He is not a man that He should relent." The tension is deliberate: YHWH genuinely responds to intercession without being fickle or changeable. The noun ra'ah ("evil, harm, disaster") refers not to moral evil but to the intended judgment/punishment. The LXX renders this as kai hilasthē kurios peri tēs kakias hēs eipen poiēsai ton laon autou ("and the Lord was propitiated concerning the evil which He said He would do to His people"), using hilasthē ("was propitiated, was appeased"), a term that enters Christian theology as the root of hilasmos ("propitiation," 1 John 2:2; 4:10) and hilastērion ("mercy seat / place of propitiation," Rom 3:25).

**mishney evreyhem mizzeh umizzeh hem ketuvim** (v. 15) -- "on both their sides, on this side and on that side, they were written." The phrase mishney evreyhem ("from both their sides") indicates that the tablets were inscribed on both front and back, maximizing the surface area for the divine text. The demonstrative mizzeh umizzeh ("from this and from that") reinforces the bilateral inscription. This detail is unique to these tablets; normal inscriptions in the ancient Near East were on one side only. The implication is either that the text was too extensive for one side, or that the divine inscription defied normal scribal convention, filling every available surface as an expression of the fullness of the divine word.

**parua... leshimtsah beqameyhem** (v. 25) -- "unrestrained... to a whispering/derision among those who rose against them." The adjective parua (from para', "to let loose, to let go, to uncover") describes a state of abandoned restraint -- the people are "let loose, running wild, uncovered" (cf. Prov 29:18, "where there is no vision the people are let loose," yippara'). The noun shimtsah (hapax legomenon, from sh-m-ts, "to whisper, to deride") indicates shameful gossip or mocking derision. The phrase beqameyhem ("among those who rose against them") refers to Israel's enemies. The complete image: Aaron's failure of leadership has reduced Israel to a state of chaotic license that will become an object of scornful gossip among hostile nations.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 32 is the dramatic center and theological crisis of the entire book of Exodus. The chapter divides into six scenes that alternate between the mountain-top and the camp, between YHWH and the people, between judgment and intercession: (1) the people's sin with the golden calf (vv. 1-6); (2) YHWH's disclosure and decree on the mountain (vv. 7-10); (3) Moses's first intercession (vv. 11-14); (4) the descent, the shattering of the tablets, and the destruction of the calf (vv. 15-20); (5) the confrontation with Aaron and the Levites' zeal (vv. 21-29); (6) Moses's second intercession and the divine verdict (vv. 30-35).

The narrative is structured by a devastating irony of simultaneous action: while YHWH is completing the covenant and delivering its written terms to Moses (31:18), the people below are violating its most fundamental stipulation. The temporal overlap between 31:18 and 32:1 is the hinge of the entire Sinai narrative. The tablets of the Testimony, written by the finger of God, descend the mountain only to be shattered at its base -- the physical destruction of the covenant document mirrors the spiritual destruction of the covenant relationship.

The chapter contains two intercessions by Moses, and their structure is carefully differentiated. The first intercession (vv. 11-13) is theological and argumentative: Moses appeals to YHWH's reputation before Egypt (v. 12), to His covenant promises to the patriarchs (v. 13), and to His investment in the exodus itself (v. 11). The second intercession (vv. 31-32) is personal and sacrificial: Moses offers himself as a substitute, his name for theirs. The first intercession succeeds in averting total destruction (v. 14); the second does not achieve full forgiveness but secures continued divine guidance (v. 34).

The shattering of the tablets (v. 19) is not an act of rage but a prophetic sign-act. Moses, who has just spent forty days in YHWH's presence receiving these tablets, destroys them deliberately at the foot of the mountain in the sight of the people. The covenant is broken -- not merely violated by the people's sin but formally annulled by the mediator's act. This annulment is a mercy: if the covenant remained in force, its curses would fall immediately and irrevocably. By shattering the tablets, Moses creates the space for covenant renewal (chs. 33-34).

The chapter's final verse (v. 35) stands as an ominous coda: even after intercession, even after judgment by the Levites, YHWH strikes the people. The verb nagaf ("to strike, to plague") leaves the nature and extent of the punishment unspecified -- a deliberate narrative restraint that allows the severity to be imagined rather than quantified. The doubled attribution -- "the calf which Aaron made" -- ensures that Aaron's culpability is not forgotten even as the chapter closes.

### Theological Notes

The golden calf narrative confronts the reader with the paradox of idolatry at its most acute: the people have not rejected YHWH but have attempted to worship YHWH through an image. Aaron's declaration "A feast to YHWH tomorrow" (v. 5) reveals that the calf is intended not as an alternative deity but as a visible representation of the invisible God. This is precisely the form of idolatry that the second commandment prohibits (Exod 20:4-6): not the worship of other gods (first commandment) but the representation of the true God through a created form. The sin of the golden calf is thus the paradigmatic violation of the second commandment, and it establishes the principle that governs all subsequent Israelite theology of worship: YHWH determines how He is to be worshipped, and no human innovation, however sincere, may substitute for divine instruction. The contrast with the preceding chapters is architecturally devastating: chapters 25-31 present YHWH's meticulous instructions for His own dwelling (the Tabernacle), while chapter 32 presents the people's unauthorized attempt to create their own representation of the divine. The Tabernacle is God's chosen form of presence; the calf is humanity's presumptuous substitute.

Moses's role in this chapter establishes the theological category of the mediator-intercessor that will shape the entire biblical tradition. His two intercessions demonstrate two distinct modes of mediation: (1) prophetic argument -- reasoning with God on the basis of His own commitments and character (vv. 11-13); and (2) substitutionary self-offering -- placing oneself in the breach between a holy God and a sinful people (v. 32). The first mode will be taken up by the prophets (cf. Amos 7:2-6; Jer 14:7-9); the second will reach its culmination in the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 and, in Christian theology, in the cross of Christ. Moses's broken sentence in v. 32 -- "if You will forgive their sin -- " -- with its unfinished protasis, is the grammar of intercessory love that cannot conceive of its own survival apart from those for whom it pleads. Paul echoes this exact posture in Romans 9:3: "I could wish that I myself were accursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my brothers."

YHWH's response to Moses's intercession (v. 14, vayyinnachem, "He relented") raises what is perhaps the central theological question of the Hebrew Bible: in what sense does YHWH "change His mind"? The formal-equivalence rendering preserves the full force of the Hebrew: nacham in the Niphal means "to be sorry, to relent, to change one's disposition." This is not a statement about divine imperfection but about divine personhood: YHWH is not an unmoved mover but a covenantal partner who responds genuinely to the words and actions of His people. The relenting is real, not merely apparent; yet it does not imply that YHWH was previously mistaken. Rather, the threat of total destruction (v. 10) and the relenting from that threat (v. 14) together reveal the complex truth that YHWH is simultaneously a God of absolute justice and a God who is "merciful and gracious, slow to anger" (34:6). The intercession of Moses does not manipulate God but participates in God's own deliberative process -- Moses's plea articulates the mercy that was always latent in the divine character.

The Levites' ordination through judgment (vv. 26-29) establishes a troubling but theologically essential principle: zeal for YHWH requires the willingness to place covenant loyalty above every other loyalty, including kinship. The phrase "each man against his son and against his brother" (v. 29) does not celebrate violence but acknowledges that faithfulness to YHWH may require the most costly renunciations. The Levites' willingness to execute judgment -- even on their own kin -- is the basis of their priestly election, and it foreshadows the radical demands of discipleship articulated by Jesus (Luke 14:26, "If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother..."). The three thousand who fell (v. 28) stand in poignant contrast to the three thousand saved on the day of Pentecost (Acts 2:41) -- a typological reversal noted by the church fathers in which the letter of the law kills but the Spirit gives life (2 Cor 3:6).

The "book" of YHWH (vv. 32-33) introduces the concept of a divine register that will become increasingly prominent in later Scripture (Ps 69:29; Isa 4:3; Dan 12:1; Mal 3:16; Phil 4:3; Rev 3:5; 20:12-15). YHWH's response to Moses -- "Whoever has sinned against Me, I will blot him out of My book" (v. 33) -- establishes the principle of individual accountability that corrects any notion of collective substitution: Moses cannot die in the people's place because each person bears responsibility for their own sin. Yet this principle does not negate the efficacy of Moses's intercession, which has already secured the nation's survival (v. 14). The tension between collective intercession and individual accountability, between Moses's offered substitution and YHWH's insistence on personal responsibility, is never fully resolved in the Hebrew Bible. It waits for a mediator who is able to bear the sin of many and yet whose righteousness is so complete that His substitution satisfies rather than circumvents divine justice.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 32 appears on pages 75-76, with the chapter beginning on page 75. At v. 1, the LXX renders the people's demand as poiēson hēmin theous hoi proporeuontai hēmōn ("make for us gods who will go before us"), closely matching the MT. The description of Moses uses the demonstrative houtos ho anthrōpos Mōusēs ("this man Moses"), paralleling the Hebrew zeh Mosheh ha'ish. At v. 4, the LXX reads kai eplasen auta en tē graphidi kai epoiēsen auto moschos chōneuton ("and he shaped them with a stylus and made it a molten calf"), where the shift from plural auta ("them," referring to the gold pieces) to singular auto ("it," the finished calf) mirrors the MT's transition. At v. 4b, the proclamation houtoi hoi theoi sou Israēl ("these are your gods, O Israel") preserves the problematic plural. At v. 6, the LXX renders letsacheq as paizein ("to play"), and this is the form Paul cites in 1 Corinthians 10:7 (epaizen). At v. 7, YHWH's words to Moses use badize katabēthi ("go, go down"), a double imperative matching the Hebrew lekh red. Notably, the LXX renders ammekha ("your people") as ho laos sou ("your people"), using laos ("people as a nation") rather than ethnos. At v. 10, hannichah li is rendered eason me ("permit me, let me be"). At v. 14, the critical verb vayyinnachem is rendered kai hilasthē kurios ("and the Lord was propitiated"), interpreting the Hebrew "relenting" as divine propitiation -- a significant theological translation that introduces sacrificial/cultic language absent from the MT. At v. 15, the description of the tablets as written "on both sides" is rendered engegrammena eis ta duo merē autōn enthen kai enthen ēsan gegrammena ("inscribed on their two sides, on this side and that side they were written"). At v. 16, the Vaticanus reads kai hē graphē graphē theou estin kekolammenē en tais plaxin ("and the writing is the writing of God, engraved on the tablets"), using kekolammenē ("engraved") for the MT's charut. At v. 25, the LXX renders parua ("let loose, unrestrained") as dieskidasmenon ("scattered, dispersed"), a different image -- not wild abandon but social disintegration. At v. 32, the incomplete protasis of the MT (im-tissa chattatam --) is completed in the LXX with aphes ("forgive"): ei men apheis autois tēn hamartian, aphes ("if you will forgive them their sin, forgive"), a syntactic smoothing that eliminates the anguished silence of the Hebrew aposiopesis. At v. 35, the final verse uses epataxen kurios ton laon ("the Lord struck the people"), rendering vayyiggof YHWH et-ha'am with patassō ("to strike, to smite"), the standard LXX verb for divine judgment.

--- LOGOS
